publications.title: Spatial Affordance-Aware Affine Transformation Between Heterogeneous Spaces for Mixed Reality Remote Collaboration
publications.abstract: We propose a spatial affordance-aware affine transformation method between heterogeneous spaces for continuous multi-object matching in shared mixed reality spaces. While previous redirection and spatial mapping approaches utilize physical objects and walkable areas, a critical gap remains in enabling continuous mapping between dissimilar physical environments that supports both precise object alignment and seamless locomotion in a shared space. Our method structurally segments heterogeneous spaces into interaction zones and constructs affine patches based on object adjacency and facing configuration, enabling continuous correspondence. We evaluate our method using a dataset of paired dissimilar spaces and demonstrate that, unlike conventional grid-based methods, our approach achieves broader spatial alignment and richer object matching. The results show that our method can serve as an effective mapping framework for shared environments requiring semantic continuity and structural coherence across diverse real-world spaces.
publications.figureCaption: Fig. 1: This figure illustrates a mixed reality remote collaboration scenario after applying the affine patch-based transformation. (a) Top-down views of heterogeneous Space A and Space B annotated with spatial affordance-aware affine patches. Avatar motion across spaces follows the scale ratios between matched patches. (b) Following user movement across matched patches, avatars move to corresponding locations and sit on mapped chairs, forming an L-shaped collaborative arrangement consistent with spatial affordances.

publications.title: Task Breakpoint Generation using Origin-Centric Graph in Virtual Reality Recordings for Adaptive Playback
publications.abstract: We propose a method for generating task breakpoints based on an Origin-Centric Graph (OCG) to segment goal-oriented activity recordings into task units for adaptive playback in virtual reality environments. With the development of AR/VR head-mounted displays, research on adaptive tutorials and authoring tools has become active, but existing task segmentation methods mainly rely on manual annotation or are restricted to 2D video, which limits their applicability to 3D VR contexts. In our approach, assembly scenarios with clearly defined task boundaries are recorded using a structured spatio-temporal scene graph, and the OCG is employed to track changes in the central object and the formation of new groups, thereby generating task breakpoints automatically. A user study collected user-perceived task breakpoints to establish ground truth, and comparison with the algorithm-detected breakpoints demonstrated high agreement and confirmed accuracy in supporting adaptive playback. The proposed task segmentation method provides a foundation for dynamically adjusting VR playback according to user proficiency and progress, with potential for extension into automatic timeline segmentation systems for diverse VR recordings.
publications.figureCaption: Fig. 1: The figure illustrates how tasks are recorded in VR and segmented into fine and coarse units through task breakpoint generation. (A) shows the recording of a drone assembly process using the STSG and the construction of the OCG. (B) presents the result of task breakpoint generation: the pink marker indicates a fine breakpoint, such as the completion of assembling a single propeller, while the yellow marker indicates a coarse breakpoint, such as the completion of assembling all four propellers as a single unit.

publications.title: VR Zen Garden: Designing a Virtual Environment for Stress Relief
publications.abstract: We propose VR Zen Garden, a virtual reality application designed to support stress relief and meditation through interactive spatial soundscapes. The system enables users to create personalized auditory environments by combining two key interactions: object placement, which spatializes sound-emitting objects, and sand pattern raking, which modulates sound properties through visual patterns. These inputs are processed by the Sound Spatializer and the Metaphoric Sound Mixer, allowing users to intuitively shape immersive 3D soundscapes in real time. VR Zen Garden offers an engaging and embodied approach to mindfulness, highlighting the potential of sound-driven design in immersive wellness applications.
publications.figureCaption: Figure 1: System Overview. (a) Representative view of the VR Zen Garden. (b) Sand pattern raking interaction (cyan). (c) Object placement interaction (magenta). (d) 2D and 3D user interface components (green). (e) Top-down view of the entire VR scene.

publications.title: Architectural Elements Augmentation from 2D Artworks for Spatial Experience of AR Exhibition
publications.abstract: We present a spatial augmentation system designed for augmented reality exhibition experiences, focusing on the immersive reinterpretation of two-dimensional artworks. Existing AR exhibition applications primarily augment content related to the artwork, but fall short of its architectural elements, which are crucial for integrating the artwork into the viewer's surrounding space and enabling immersive spatial alignment. This study proposes a spatial augmentation approach that takes an artwork image as input, generates floor patterns and architectural 3D models through 2D and 3D model generation pipelines, and semantically aligns and augments them in the physical space. This spatial augmentation intends to enhance viewer engagement and perceived presence by maintaining spatial coherence between virtual and real space. By activating architectural surfaces as part of the experience, the system repositions AR exhibitions as spatial narratives rather than conventional overlay-driven displays.
publications.figureCaption: Figure 1: Conceptual and implementation visualization of the proposed system. (a) A user views a physical artwork in an indoor space. (b) Architectural elements (walls, ceiling, floor) are extracted from the artwork to generate virtual 2D/3D assets. (c) Real-time view showing seamless spatial augmentation without occluding physical objects.

publications.title: Human-Scene Interaction Data Generation with Virtual Environment using User-Centric Scene Graph
publications.abstract: We present a new approach using virtual reality to capture human-scene interaction data within 3D environments. Traditional methods required costly physical setups with real furniture, making it difficult to collect both interaction and 3D scene data comprehensively. Our solution implements a user-centric scene graph with a hierarchical structure that captures both user interactions and 3D scene elements. The resulting dataset contains real human interaction data, dynamic scene information, synthetic sensor data, and scene graph annotations. This work provides a foundation for developing mixed reality experiences using 3D scene graphs with human interaction data.
publications.figureCaption: Figure 1: The overview of sequential human-scene interaction data generation pipeline. (i) Human-scene interaction data were collected using VR HMDs and controllers. (ii) Hand-object interaction collection. (iii) Data augmentation is performed in three ways: hand pose augmentation, background augmentation, and scene generation.

publications.title: Meta-Objects: Interactive and Multisensory Virtual Objects Learned From the Real World for Use in Augmented Reality
publications.abstract: We introduce the concept of a meta-object, a next-generation virtual object that inherits the form, properties, and functions of its real-world counterpart, enabling seamless synchronization, interaction, and sharing between the physical and virtual worlds. While many virtual objects today provide partial sensory feedback and dynamic behavior, meta-objects fully integrate interactive and multisensory features within a structured data framework to enable real-time immersive experiences in a postmetaverse intelligent simulation platform. Property-embedded modeling, adaptive multisensory feedback, and scene graph-based intelligence simulation together support scalable ecosystem integration across reality and virtuality.
publications.figureCaption: FIGURE 1. Difference between a meta-object and a virtual object is that it inherits the form, properties, and functions from a corresponding physical object, and it can affect the counterpart physical object. The virtual avatar of the real user could manipulate the real object in remote reality by interacting with a meta-object in the metaverse.

publications.title: Mutual Space Representation Standardization for Mixed and Augmented Reality Remote Collaboration
publications.abstract: This paper proposes a mutual space representation to support remote collaboration in mixed and augmented reality environments. To achieve high immersion and coexistence in MAR remote collaboration, a mutual space based on heterogeneous physical spaces that can be commonly utilized by collaborating participants is required. Although previous studies have proposed various spatial matching and modifying methods to overcome spatial heterogeneity, their different representations have made their integrated use in real-world scenarios difficult. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes a mutual space representation standard that includes the representation of physical space, matching space, and collaboration context required for mutual space generation and management. We expect that the proposed standards will serve as a foundation for seamlessly applying solutions accumulated in academia to industrial systems, thereby providing users with a more immersive and coexistent MAR remote collaboration experience.
publications.figureCaption: Figure 1: Figure 1 illustrates (A) the hierarchical structure of the proposed mutual space representation and (B) an application scenario. In this example, two users collaborate in remote environments: User 1 in Virtual Reality (VR) and User 2 in Augmented Reality (AR). Scene 1, associated with User 1, consists of two rooms and three clusters, whereas Scene 2, associated with User 2, consists of one room and one cluster.

publications.title: Experience Graph using Spatio-Temporal Scene Data for Replaying Mixed Reality Interaction
publications.abstract: This study proposes an experience graph, a mixed reality experience replaying method for recording and replaying spatially adaptive interaction in a mixed reality environment. Many MR immersive interactions are experienced in a space different from the original recorded space, so it is important to adaptively experience the interaction in the local space. Prior work on scene generation or adaptive object placement addressed collision and mismatch in local spaces, but did not consider how space-dependent interactions change and how the resulting experience is transformed. This study structures spatial information and dependent interactions into an experience graph and proposes a spatially adaptive replay method that minimizes interaction deformation. Recorded interactions are evaluated quantitatively through loss rate and transformation rate, and replayed interactions are evaluated qualitatively through user experiments. The study ultimately reflects indicators important to user experience in the experience graph to support immersive spatial MR experiences that can be actively recorded and shared beyond the limitations of time and space.
publications.figureCaption: Figure 1: Scene graph using both geometric and semantic information of objects from progressed research.

publications.title: Object Cluster Registration of Dissimilar Rooms Using Geometric Spatial Affordance Graph to Generate Shared Virtual Spaces
publications.abstract: We propose Object Cluster Registration using Geometric Spatial Affordance Graph to support user interaction with multiple objects in a shared space generated from two dissimilar rooms. Previous research on generating a shared virtual space from dissimilar real spaces has only reflected individual object information and aimed at maximizing the area of the shared space. This led to limited interactions that relied on singular object affordances while neglecting the usability and effectiveness of the generated shared spaces. The proposed method considers the relationship between objects based on facing formation, extracts optimal object-cluster pairs, and aligns dissimilar rooms when generating shared virtual spaces. In evaluation with multi-cluster space pairs, applying object-cluster registration with geometric spatial affordance graph preserved object correlations more effectively without significantly reducing shared space size.
publications.figureCaption: Figure 1: This study proposes a shared virtual space considering optimal object clusters through Object Cluster Registration (OCR) using Geometric Spatial Affordance Graph (GSAG). (a) is the shared virtual space created when applying the existing method that aims to maximize the area without OCR and the proposed OCR using GSAG. (b) is registered objects for multi-object interaction in each method. When OCR is used, more and wider co-interactable objects (highlighted green box) are registered as the same cluster.

publications.title: Spatial Affordance-aware Interactable Subspace Allocation for Mixed Reality Telepresence
publications.abstract: To enable remote virtual reality and augmented reality clients to collaborate as if they were in the same space during mixed reality telepresence, it is essential to overcome spatial heterogeneity and generate a unified shared collaborative environment by integrating remote spaces into a target host space. Especially when multiple remote users connect, a large shared space is necessary for people to maintain their personal space while collaborating, but existing simple intersection methods lead to narrow shared spaces as the number of remote spaces increases. To robustly align to the host space even as the number of remote spaces increases, we propose a spatial affordance-aware interactable subspace allocation algorithm. The key concept of our approach is to consider perceivable and interactable areas separately, where every user views the same mutual space but each remote user has a different interactable subspace based on location and spatial affordance.
publications.figureCaption: Figure 1: (A) All users can see the same AR host's space but have different interactable subspaces for robust mutual space generation as the number of remote clients increases: AR host (red), AR client (yellow), and VR client (blue). (B) Remote AR/VR clients access the host's space through their avatar, and their interactable subspaces and positions are determined in each context.

publications.title: The Effects of Spatial Configuration on Relative Translation Gain Thresholds in Redirected Walking
publications.abstract: In this study, we explore how spatial configurations can be reflected in determining the threshold range of relative translation gains, a translation gain-based redirected walking technique that scales the user's movement in virtual reality in different ratios for width and depth. While previous work showed that various cognitive factors or individual differences influence redirected walking thresholds, constructive studies on how environmental composition affects these thresholds through visual perception were lacking. We examined the effect of spatial configurations on relative translation gain thresholds by analyzing participant responses and gaze distribution data in two user studies. The first study concerned room size and the existence of objects, and the second focused on the combined impact of room size and spatial layout. Our results show that room size, object existence, and spatial layout significantly affect the threshold range and can inform adaptive virtual-space rescaling guidelines.
publications.figureCaption: Fig. 6 Screenshot of the eight experimental conditions in Study 2. The inner floor area expressed in the Large condition is the same size as the Small conditions' entire floor area and is located at the center of the room. Combined paths and exhibit objects are placed in it.

publications.title: Bring Store in My Room: AR Store Authoring System for Spatial Experience in Mobile Shopping
publications.abstract: We propose an AR store authoring system that enables users to customize a physical space to promote spatial experience in mobile AR shopping. Current AR shopping research mainly enhances the shopping experience by presenting virtual items in the user's space. However, since stores and products are inseparable in creating a holistic shopping experience, we aim to encompass both elements. Instead of simply augmenting a whole store, this study introduces a space-adaptive AR store that divides a virtual store into multiple sections and places them onto physical space according to labeled surfaces. This prototype mobile application is intended to deliver a complete spatial shopping experience by allowing users to customize stores in their own space and ultimately attach store experience to the AR shopping system.
publications.figureCaption: Figure 1: Systematic flow of the space alignment and AR store placement.

publications.title: TranSlider: User-Controlled Augmented Content Presence Visualization in AR Collaboration
publications.abstract: We present a user interface method called TranSlider, designed for AR collaboration with the concept of customized visualization of user-selected augmented content. In AR, virtual content is augmented on top of the real world, so collaborating users are inevitably exposed to diverse and overlapping information layers. Because human recognition capacity is limited, overloaded information can burden understanding and make it difficult to manipulate occluded objects. To solve this issue, we designed TranSlider to let users control the presence of augmented content by making it transparent or highlighted based on interaction. The prototype includes UI components, a manipulation function, and a network-connected collaboration module, and its applicability was verified through a preliminary interview.
publications.figureCaption: Figure 1: (A) It illustrates the main idea of the TranSlider interface: Users can control and more effectively achieve the augmented content's information in a collaborative AR scene. Participating users change each object's augmentation level and share their scene with other users; (B) Interaction process of the TranSlider: The initial state indicates the local user's limited cognitive area with a red circle. After selecting the desired target with a bare-hand gesture, the user can freely control its presence through the system.
